options: Write a sales-to-CS internal handoff note. Deal details: RetailCo, $72K ARR, 3-year contract, 50 users, use case: inventory reporting. Format as a structured note, not prose.
Write an internal handoff note from sales to CS. Cover why the customer bought, what we promised, who the main contacts are, and any risks we should know about.
Write a deal handoff note for CS. Include the use case, what was committed, key stakeholders, and early warning signs.
hint: Separating the handoff into specific named fields (pain, commitments, risks, contacts, success criteria) prevents critical deal context from being buried in prose that the CSM ignores.

title: Write feature adoption email campaign
originalPrompt: A CS team wants to drive adoption of an underused analytics feature among existing customers.
options: Write a 3-email adoption campaign for an underused analytics feature. Email 1 (Day 0): problem-awareness (why manual reporting costs time). Email 2 (Day 7): feature education (what it does + how to enable it). Email 3 (Day 14): social proof (customer quote + result metric) + CTA. Each email: max 100 words, subject line, one CTA. Specify trigger logic for each email.
Write 3 emails to drive adoption of our analytics feature. Include awareness, education, and social proof emails. Keep them short with clear CTAs.
Create a 3-part email campaign to promote our analytics feature. First email explains the value, second shows how to use it, third uses a customer story to encourage adoption.
hint: Specifying a distinct psychological angle per email, a 100-word cap, and requiring trigger logic produces a drip campaign that the marketing automation team can configure directly.

title: Write A/B test hypothesis for landing page
originalPrompt: I want to A/B test our landing page. Can you help me think through the hypothesis?
options: Write an A/B test hypothesis for a SaaS landing page CTA button. Format: 'We believe changing [variable: CTA text from 'Get a Demo' to 'See It in Action'] will [direction: increase] [metric: demo request click-through rate] because [rationale: action-oriented language reduces perceived commitment].' Include: expected lift (%), minimum detectable effect, and suggested test duration at 2,000 visitors/week.
Write an A/B test hypothesis for changing our CTA button text. Explain what we're testing, what we expect to happen, and why.
Write an A/B test hypothesis for our landing page CTA. Use the standard format with variable, metric, and rationale. Include expected lift and test duration.
hint: Filling in the hypothesis template with specific values and requiring MDE and duration prevents endless inconclusive tests by forcing statistical planning upfront.

title: Write retargeting ad copy sequence
originalPrompt: A growth marketer needs retargeting ad copy for visitors who viewed the pricing page but didn't convert.
options: Write 3 retargeting ad variants for pricing page visitors who didn't convert. Ad 1: social proof (customer logo + stat). Ad 2: objection handler (address price concern with ROI framing). Ad 3: urgency (limited-time trial offer). Each ad: headline (max 25 chars), body (max 90 chars), and CTA label. Specify the audience segment each ad targets (recency: 1-day, 7-day, 30-day).
Write retargeting ads for visitors who saw our pricing page. Cover social proof, pricing objections, and a trial offer. Keep them short.
Create 3 retargeting ad variants for pricing page abandoners. Use different angles: proof, ROI framing, and urgency. Include headline, body, and CTA.
hint: Assigning a recency segment to each ad angle is the key differentiation—it turns 3 generic variants into a sequenced retargeting strategy.

title: Write PLG activation email sequence
originalPrompt: Help me write onboarding emails for our free trial users. We want them to activate and upgrade.
options: Write a 5-email PLG activation sequence for free trial users (14-day trial). Email triggers and purpose: Day 0 (welcome + first action CTA), Day 1 if no action (nudge + video resource), Day 3 (feature spotlight on the activation milestone), Day 7 (mid-trial check-in + ROI framing), Day 12 (upgrade urgency + comparison to paid tier). Each email: subject line, 80-word max body, and one CTA. Tone: helpful, not pushy.
Write a 5-email trial sequence. Cover welcome, feature education, mid-trial check-in, and upgrade push. Keep emails short with clear calls to action.
Create a trial onboarding email sequence for our PLG product. Map each email to a point in the 14-day trial. Use behaviour triggers where relevant.
hint: Specifying behavioural triggers (Day 1 if no action), an 80-word cap, and a distinct purpose per email produces a sequence that a marketing automation team can configure and deploy.

title: Write webinar registration page copy
originalPrompt: Write copy for a webinar registration page about AI in B2B sales.
options: Write webinar registration page copy. Topic: 'How B2B Sales Teams Use AI to Cut Research Time by 50%'. Include: headline (max 8 words), 3 bullet 'you'll learn' points (max 10 words each), speaker bio placeholder (1 sentence), and registration CTA (3 words). Total: max 80 words.

Write the copy for a webinar registration page about using AI in B2B sales. Include a compelling headline that captures the value of the webinar, three bullet points explaining what attendees will learn, a short bio section for the speaker, and a strong call to action button. The copy should be persuasive and make prospects want to sign up to attend the webinar.
Webinar: AI in sales. Learn how. Register now.
hint: Defining word caps per copy element and a total word budget produces registration page copy that fits a real landing page template without resizing.

title: Analyse conversion funnel drop-off
originalPrompt: A growth analyst needs to identify where to focus optimisation effort in a leaky acquisition funnel.
options: Analyse this conversion funnel data and identify the highest-leverage drop-off point: Ad click (10,000) → Landing page visit (6,500) → Sign-up page view (2,100) → Trial sign-up (420) → Activated user (105). For each step: calculate conversion rate, benchmark it against SaaS industry averages, and flag whether it's above/below benchmark. Rank the 3 drop-off points by absolute volume lost. Recommend where to focus first with rationale.
Look at our funnel data and tell me where we're losing the most people. Explain what's happening at each step and what we should fix.
Analyse this funnel: show conversion rates at each step, compare to benchmarks, and tell me where to focus optimisation efforts first.
hint: Providing the raw numbers inline, requiring benchmark comparison, and asking for absolute volume ranking produces a funnel analysis that prioritises by impact, not just conversion rate.

title: Write growth loop design document
originalPrompt: Help me design a growth loop for our product. I want to understand how growth compounds.
options: Design a viral/product-led growth loop for a B2B project management tool. Map the loop: (1) trigger (user invites a collaborator), (2) action (collaborator receives a task notification), (3) value moment (collaborator completes a task in the product), (4) reward/output (user sees team activity feed update), (5) loop closes (collaborator becomes an active user who invites others). For each loop node: identify the friction to reduce and the metric to instrument.
Design a growth loop for our project management product. Show how one user's action leads to new users. Cover the key steps and what we should measure.
Map the viral growth loop for our tool. Start with a user action that triggers a new user to join. Show the cycle and the metrics for each step.
hint: Labelling each loop node with a specific metric to instrument and a friction to reduce produces a growth loop spec that an engineering team can actually instrument and optimise.

title: Write paid channel budget allocation strategy
originalPrompt: A growth director needs to allocate a $50K/month paid acquisition budget across channels for a B2B SaaS product.
options: Create a $50K/month paid acquisition budget allocation for a B2B SaaS targeting operations managers. Channels to consider: Google Search, LinkedIn Ads, G2/review sites, content syndication, and retargeting. For each channel: recommended budget allocation, rationale (CAC benchmark, intent signal), expected lead volume at 3% CTR baseline, and one risk. Output as a table with a bottom-line CAC estimate.
Allocate a $50K paid marketing budget across Google, LinkedIn, and retargeting. Include rationale and expected results for each channel.
Build a paid acquisition budget plan for $50K/month. Cover Google Search, LinkedIn, review sites, and retargeting. Show expected leads and CAC per channel.
hint: Requiring a risk per channel and a bottom-line CAC estimate forces the allocation to be grounded in performance accountability, not just budget distribution.

title: Write brand voice guidelines
originalPrompt: Write brand voice guidelines for our company. We want everyone to sound consistent when they write.
options: Write brand voice guidelines for a B2B SaaS company targeting SMB owners. Include: 4 voice attributes (each with a 1-sentence description, a 'we are / we are not' contrast, and a writing example). Add a section on tone shifts by context (website vs support vs social). Max 400 words.
Write brand voice guidelines. Include our tone, personality attributes, and examples of how to write in our voice. Cover different contexts like marketing and customer support.
Create brand voice guidelines for our SaaS company. Define 4 personality attributes with do/don't examples and how the tone changes depending on the channel.
hint: The 'we are / we are not' contrast per attribute is the key differentiator—it gives writers clear guardrails that purely positive descriptions never provide.

title: Write press release for product launch
originalPrompt: A PR manager needs to write a press release for a major product launch announcement.
options: Write a press release for the launch of an AI analytics feature in a B2B SaaS product. Structure: dateline + headline (benefit-led, max 12 words), lead paragraph (who/what/why/when, max 50 words), product quote from CEO, 2 key feature bullets, boilerplate (1 sentence), and press contact. AP style. Avoid 'revolutionary', 'game-changing', and 'cutting-edge'.
Write a press release for our AI analytics feature launch. Include a headline, CEO quote, feature highlights, and contact info. Keep it professional.
Draft a product launch press release. Cover what we're announcing, why it matters, a quote from our CEO, and key features. Follow standard press release format.
hint: Banning the three most overused PR adjectives and specifying AP style forces a press release that journalists will actually read rather than immediately delete.

title: Write internal comms for layoff announcement
originalPrompt: I need to write an internal communication about some layoffs at our company. It needs to be sensitive and professional.
options: Write an internal company announcement for a 10% workforce reduction. Tone: direct, empathetic, no euphemisms. Structure: (1) what is happening and why (market context, 2 sentences), (2) who is affected and the process (timeline, severance, support), (3) message to remaining team (what stays the same, leadership's commitment), (4) Q&A process. Avoid: 'rightsizing', 'difficult decision', passive voice. Length: 300 words.
Write a layoff announcement email for the company. Cover why it's happening, who is affected, and what support we're offering. Be empathetic but direct.
Draft a company-wide layoff communication. Explain the business reasons, describe the support for affected employees, and reassure the remaining team. Avoid corporate jargon.
hint: Banning specific euphemisms ('rightsizing') and passive voice, combined with a structured 4-section format, produces a communication that treats affected employees with genuine dignity.

title: Write CEO LinkedIn bio
originalPrompt: Write a LinkedIn bio for our CEO to use on their profile.
options: Write a LinkedIn 'About' section for a B2B SaaS CEO. Max 150 words. Include: what the company does and who it helps (1 sentence), what the CEO built before this (1-2 sentences), their leadership philosophy (1 sentence), and a CTA to connect. First-person. No clichés like 'passionate' or 'visionary'.

Write a LinkedIn bio for our CEO. Cover their background, what they're building now, their leadership approach, and a way for people to connect with them. It should be engaging and personal, written in the first person. Highlight their achievements and what makes them unique as a leader in the B2B SaaS space. Make it compelling for potential investors, partners, and customers who might visit their LinkedIn profile.
CEO bio: founder of X, building SaaS product, passionate leader. Connect with me.
hint: Banning 'passionate' and 'visionary', specifying each content element, and capping at 150 words produces an authentic bio that stands out on LinkedIn.

title: Write crisis communications holding statement
originalPrompt: A communications team needs to prepare a holding statement for a potential data breach before full details are confirmed.
options: Write a data breach holding statement for a B2B SaaS company to be issued within 2 hours of a suspected incident. Requirements: acknowledge we are investigating a potential security incident (without confirming breach), state what we are doing right now (3 specific actions), commit to an update within 24 hours, provide a direct contact for affected customers. Max 100 words. Legal-reviewed tone (factual, no admissions).
Write a holding statement for a potential data breach. Acknowledge the incident, explain what we're doing, and tell customers when they'll hear more.
Draft a crisis holding statement for a suspected data breach. Be transparent, tell customers what we know and don't know, what actions we're taking, and when the next update will be.
hint: Specifying 'no admissions' and requiring three concrete current actions produces a legally defensible holding statement that still demonstrates accountability.

title: Write investor narrative for fundraising deck
originalPrompt: Help me write the narrative for our fundraising deck. We're raising a Series A and I need it to be compelling.
options: Write the narrative arc for a Series A fundraising deck (B2B SaaS, $2M ARR, 3x YoY growth, targeting ops teams). Slide-by-slide narrative: Problem → Why Now → Solution → Traction → Market Size → Business Model → Team → Use of Funds → Ask. For each slide: 1-sentence core claim, the key data point to include, and the investor concern each slide must preemptively address. Format as a slide narrative guide.
Write a Series A fundraising deck narrative. Cover the problem, solution, traction, market, team, and use of funds. Make each slide compelling with data.
Create a fundraising deck narrative for our Series A. Map the story arc from problem to ask. Include what data to show on each slide and what investor questions to address.
hint: Requiring a 'preemptive investor concern' per slide is the key differentiator—it forces the narrative to answer objections before they are raised, which is what closes funding rounds.

title: Write thought leadership op-ed outline
originalPrompt: A founder wants to write an op-ed for a trade publication on the future of remote work technology.
options: Write an op-ed outline for a trade publication on 'Why remote work technology is failing distributed teams—and what needs to change.' Target: 800 words. Structure: provocative thesis (1 sentence), 3 supporting arguments (each with evidence hook and counterpoint), a solution section (2-3 practical recommendations), and a closing call to action for the industry. Identify 2 places to insert original data or proprietary research.
Write an op-ed outline about remote work technology. Include a strong thesis, main arguments, and recommendations. Target around 800 words.
Create an op-ed outline on the future of remote work tech. Open with a strong take, build the argument with evidence, and close with recommendations.
hint: Requiring a counterpoint per argument and flagging where to insert original data produces an op-ed outline that will pass editorial review at a serious trade publication.

title: Write conference speaking abstract
originalPrompt: Write a speaking abstract for a tech conference submission about AI-powered customer support.
options: Write a conference speaking abstract for a SaaStr-style B2B event. Topic: 'How we reduced support ticket volume by 60% with AI triage'. Max 150 words. Include: session title (provocative, stat-led), 3 audience takeaways as bullet points, speaker credential (1 sentence). No buzzwords.

Write a conference abstract for a talk about using AI in customer support. Describe what the talk covers, what the audience will learn, and who the speaker is. The talk is about how our company implemented AI-powered ticket triage and routing, which significantly reduced the volume of tickets that needed human intervention. Include some compelling statistics about the results we achieved and make the abstract engaging enough that conference organizers will want to select this session.
Abstract: AI support triage, 60% ticket reduction, three takeaways.
hint: A stat-led title and a 150-word cap with three prescribed elements produce an abstract that conference programme committees rank higher than abstract narrative descriptions.

title: Write company values statement
originalPrompt: Write our company values. We want to define what we stand for and how we work.
options: Write 4 company values for a 50-person B2B SaaS startup. For each value: a 2-3 word label, a 1-sentence definition, and one concrete example of what it looks like in practice (not 'we believe in X'). Values should be specific enough that an employee could use them to make a real decision. Avoid generic values like 'integrity' or 'excellence' without context.
Write company values for our startup. Include 4-5 values with names and descriptions. Make them genuine and reflect how we actually work.
Create 4 company values. Each should have a short name and an explanation of what it means. Give an example of each value in action.
hint: Requiring a concrete 'looks like in practice' example per value and banning empty generic labels forces values that employees can actually use as decision-making guides.

title: Write M&A integration communication
originalPrompt: A VP of Communications needs to draft internal and external communications for a company acquisition announcement.
options: Write a communications plan for a B2B SaaS acquisition announcement. Cover 4 audiences with separate messages: (1) acquired company employees (tone: reassuring, what changes/stays same), (2) acquiring company employees (tone: excited, strategic rationale), (3) customers of both companies (tone: value-focused, no disruption), (4) press release. For each: key message (1 sentence), FAQs to anticipate (3 per audience), and recommended channel and timing.
Write acquisition announcement communications for employees, customers, and press. Tailor each for the audience and cover what they need to know.
Draft M&A communications for 4 audiences: acquired employees, our employees, shared customers, and the press. Include key messages, FAQs, and timing for each.
hint: Requiring 3 FAQs per audience and specifying channel and timing forces the communications plan to anticipate real stakeholder concerns rather than just crafting messages.

title: Write nurture email for MQL who went cold
originalPrompt: Write a re-engagement email for a lead who downloaded our whitepaper 30 days ago but hasn't responded to any follow-up.
options: Write a re-engagement email for an MQL who downloaded a whitepaper 30 days ago and went silent after 2 follow-ups. Angle: share a new, relevant resource (not the same whitepaper). Max 80 words. Include: subject line that doesn't reference previous emails, one specific value-add (a link to a relevant case study or short video), and a low-friction CTA (reply with a yes/no question).
Write a re-engagement email for a cold lead. Share something new and useful. Keep it short and don't pressure them.
Write an email to re-engage a lead who downloaded our whitepaper. Use a new piece of content as the hook and ask a simple question at the end.
hint: A subject line that doesn't reference previous emails avoids the passive-aggressive tone that makes cold leads unsubscribe rather than re-engage.

title: Write newsletter introduction section
originalPrompt: A content team needs to write the opening section of a weekly B2B SaaS newsletter.
options: Write the intro section of a weekly B2B SaaS newsletter. Max 80 words. Structure: 1-sentence hook (surprising stat or counterintuitive claim relevant to the week's topic), 2-sentence preview of what's inside, 1-sentence CTA to keep reading. First-person plural ('we'). No 'hope you're having a great week' openers.
Write the opening for our weekly newsletter. Include a hook, preview of the content, and encourage people to read on. Keep it brief.
Write a newsletter intro. Start with something interesting to hook the reader, preview the topics covered, and make them want to read more.
hint: Banning generic openers like 'hope you're having a great week' and requiring a surprising hook increases open-to-click rates by capturing attention in the email preview pane.

title: Write event promotional tweet
originalPrompt: Write a tweet promoting a free B2B webinar about scaling sales teams.
options: Write a promotional tweet for a free B2B webinar. Max 240 characters. Include: topic (scaling sales from 3 to 30 reps), one specific takeaway attendees get, date/time, and registration link placeholder. Hashtags: max 2.

Write a tweet promoting our free webinar on scaling B2B sales teams. It should be engaging and encourage people to register. Mention what they'll learn and include the date, time, and a link to sign up. Add some relevant hashtags to reach more people in the sales and B2B community. Make sure it fits in a tweet.
Free webinar: scale your sales team. Register now. #sales
hint: Requiring one specific takeaway in the tweet text proves value before asking for a registration click, which drives higher sign-up rates.

title: Write gated content landing page
originalPrompt: Write a landing page for a gated eBook. I want people to fill in the form and download it.
options: Write copy for a gated eBook landing page. eBook: '2025 State of B2B Procurement Report'. Include: H1 (benefit-led, max 8 words), 3-bullet 'what you'll learn' section (max 12 words each), social proof line (X companies downloaded), form field rationale sentence (why we ask for company size), and a submit button label (not 'Submit'). Total: max 120 words.
Write a landing page for our gated eBook on B2B procurement. Include a headline, bullet points about what readers will learn, social proof, and a form with a CTA button.
Write gated content landing page copy. Headline should be compelling, bullet points should preview the content, include a trust signal, and end with a strong form CTA.
hint: Including a 'form field rationale sentence' reduces form abandonment by addressing the privacy concern before the visitor reaches the form.

title: Write product category definition for SEO
originalPrompt: A content marketer needs to define the product category in the website's core SEO page to own the category keyword.
options: Write a 100-word 'what is [category]' SEO section for a page targeting the keyword 'revenue operations software'. Include: a plain-language definition, 3 key problems it solves for B2B companies, and a mention of who uses it. Optimise for a featured snippet (direct answer in the first sentence). No jargon.
Write a definition of revenue operations software for our SEO page. Explain what it is, who uses it, and what problems it solves. Make it search-friendly.
Write the 'what is RevOps software' section for our website. Define the category in a way that answers common search queries and helps us rank for the term.
hint: Explicitly writing for a featured snippet (direct answer in the first sentence) is the single highest-leverage SEO tactic for category definition pages.

title: Build content pillar strategy document
originalPrompt: Help me build a content strategy for our blog. We want to rank for important keywords and build authority.
options: Design a content pillar strategy for a B2B SaaS company in the HR tech space. Create 3 content pillars: each with a pillar page topic, target keyword cluster (5 keywords), 5 supporting cluster articles, and the buyer journey stage it targets (awareness/consideration/decision). Include internal linking logic between pillar and cluster pages. Output as a content architecture table.
Build a content pillar strategy for our HR tech blog. Define 3 pillars, list the keywords for each, and outline supporting articles.
Create a content strategy using topic clusters. Define 3 pillar topics with keyword clusters and supporting articles. Show how the pieces link together.
hint: Requiring internal linking logic between pillar and cluster pages is what turns a content list into an SEO architecture—without it, the cluster articles leak link equity.

title: Write product feature comparison bullet
originalPrompt: Write a bullet point comparing our product's reporting feature to a competitor's for a comparison page.
options: Write one feature comparison bullet for a 'vs competitor' page. Topic: reporting. Our advantage: real-time dashboards vs competitor's daily refresh. Max 20 words. Lead with our advantage. Factual, no superlatives.

Write a bullet point for a competitor comparison page about our reporting feature. Explain how our real-time dashboard is better than the competitor's daily data refresh. Make it compelling and persuasive so visitors understand why our approach is superior. Keep it concise and focused on the key advantage.
Real-time reports vs. daily refresh. We're faster.
hint: A 20-word cap with a 'lead with our advantage' rule and a no-superlatives constraint produces comparison copy that is credible and scannable.

title: Write ABM content personalisation brief
originalPrompt: A marketing team needs to personalise content for a named account campaign targeting 20 enterprise accounts in the retail industry.
options: Write an ABM content personalisation brief for an enterprise retail account campaign. Specify: (1) personalisation variables per account tier (company name, industry pain, recent news hook), (2) which content assets to personalise (email, landing page hero, LinkedIn ad), (3) the personalisation token structure for each asset, (4) what requires human review vs can be auto-generated. Output as a personalisation playbook.
Write a personalisation brief for our ABM campaign targeting retail accounts. Cover what to personalise, which assets, and how.
Create an ABM personalisation strategy for the retail campaign. Define which fields to personalise in emails and ads, and flag what needs manual review.
hint: Distinguishing 'auto-generated vs human review' requirements prevents the campaign from sending AI-hallucinated account details that damage enterprise relationships.

title: Write podcast guest pitch email
originalPrompt: Write an email pitching a podcast host to have our founder as a guest. Make it compelling so they say yes.
options: Write a podcast guest pitch email on behalf of a B2B SaaS founder. Target podcast: a 50k-listener B2B marketing show. Email structure: personalised observation about a recent episode (1 sentence), founder's unique angle (what counterintuitive insight they'd bring that fits the show's audience), social proof (company metric or past media appearance), and a low-friction ask (availability to chat, not a booking link). Max 100 words.
Write a pitch email to a podcast host about having our founder on the show. Mention a recent episode, explain why she'd be a great guest, and include some credentials.
Write a guest pitch email for a B2B podcast. Personalise it, explain what value our founder brings, mention their credentials, and ask for a conversation.
hint: Framing the founder's contribution as a 'counterintuitive insight that fits the show's audience' shows the host you've done the research and makes the pitch about their listeners, not the founder's exposure.

title: Write product launch email subject variants
originalPrompt: Write email subject lines for a major annual product launch.
options: Write 4 email subject lines for a major annual product launch. Max 45 characters each. One per angle: (1) year in review + what's new, (2) biggest single new feature, (3) customer quote-led, (4) urgency/event-based. Include preview text (max 85 chars) for each pair.

Write subject lines and preview text for our annual product launch email. We need several variants to test. Cover different angles including the new features we're launching, customer testimonials, and any urgency around the launch event or timing. Each subject line should be short enough to display fully on mobile and the preview text should add context without repeating the subject.
Annual launch email subjects: new features, customer love, urgency.
hint: Pairing each subject line with preview text in the brief ensures the two work together, since many recipients decide whether to open based on both fields simultaneously.

title: Write LinkedIn InMail for C-suite prospect
originalPrompt: Write a LinkedIn InMail message targeting a CFO at a large enterprise company about our finance automation product.
options: Write a LinkedIn InMail to a CFO at a 2,000-person manufacturing company about a finance process automation tool. Max 150 words. Structure: personalised opener referencing their company or a recent announcement (1 sentence), the financial inefficiency our tool addresses (1 sentence with a relevant stat), a specific outcome a similar company achieved (1 sentence), and a low-commitment CTA (share a 2-minute overview video, not a meeting ask).
Write a LinkedIn InMail to a CFO about our finance automation product. Personalise it, explain the value, mention a customer result, and ask for a call.
Write a CFO-targeted LinkedIn message. Reference their company, explain how we help with finance processes, and offer a short video or conversation.
hint: A 2-minute video CTA has 3x higher acceptance rates than a meeting request as the first ask from a cold InMail to a C-suite executive.

title: Write enterprise sales qualification framework
originalPrompt: A head of sales needs a qualification framework that sales reps can use consistently to assess enterprise opportunities.
options: Create a MEDDPICC qualification framework template for enterprise B2B sales. For each element (Metrics, Economic Buyer, Decision Criteria, Decision Process, Identify Pain, Champion, Competition), provide: a definition (1 sentence), 2 qualifying questions for the rep to ask, and a red flag that signals the deal is unqualified on that dimension. Format as a sales qualification card.
Write an enterprise sales qualification framework using MEDDPICC. Cover each letter, what it means, and questions to ask prospects.
Create a MEDDPICC template for our sales team. Define each element, give example questions, and note warning signs that a deal might not be qualified.
hint: Requiring a 'red flag' indicator per MEDDPICC element produces a qualification framework that helps reps disqualify bad deals quickly, not just qualify good ones.

title: Write voicemail script for cold outreach
originalPrompt: Write a voicemail script for a sales rep calling a cold prospect. Make it so they call back.
options: Write a cold call voicemail script (max 25 seconds / ~60 words). Structure: name and company (5 words), one specific reason for calling referenced to their role or company (1 sentence), a compelling reason to call back (one benefit or insight), and callback number said twice. No 'I'll also follow up by email' hedge. Conversational tone.
Write a voicemail script for a cold call. Introduce the rep and company, explain why they're calling, and give a reason to call back. Keep it short.
Write a 20-second voicemail script. Say who you are, mention a relevant business problem, and leave a callback number. Don't make it salesy.
hint: Banning the 'I'll also follow up by email' line forces the rep to make the voicemail compelling enough to stand on its own, which improves callback rates.

title: Write deal summary for CRM update
originalPrompt: Write a deal summary to update in our CRM after a discovery call.
options: Write a CRM deal summary after a discovery call. Max 80 words. Cover: (1) prospect's stated pain (their words), (2) business impact of the pain (metric if given), (3) decision timeline, (4) next step agreed, (5) one open question to address. Use note-style prose, not bullet points.

Write a CRM note summarising a discovery call. Cover what the prospect told us about their pain points, their timeline for a decision, and what we agreed to do next. Include any open questions that need to be answered before we can move forward. Keep it clear and useful for the account executive who will pick this up.
Discovery call summary: pain, timeline, next step.
hint: Including the prospect's stated pain in their own words (not paraphrased) is the most important CRM field—it prevents the rep from projecting assumptions into subsequent calls.

title: Write competitive battle card
originalPrompt: A sales enablement team needs to write a battle card for reps to use when competing against a specific named competitor.
options: Write a competitive battle card against Competitor X for a B2B CRM product. Sections: (1) when you'll see them (deal types and company profiles), (2) their 3 strengths (honest assessment), (3) their 3 weaknesses from a customer perspective, (4) our top 3 differentiators vs them, (5) how to handle their top 2 traps ('Their pricing is lower' and 'They have more integrations'). One-page format. No spin—include real weaknesses.
Write a competitive battle card. Cover competitor strengths and weaknesses, our differentiators, and how to handle common objections about them.
Create a battle card for competing against a major CRM competitor. Explain when we face them, what they're good and bad at, how we're different, and how to counter their sales tactics.
hint: Requiring an honest assessment of competitor strengths makes the battle card credible—reps who discover the AI downplayed a real competitor strength stop trusting the card entirely.

title: Write territory plan for new region
originalPrompt: Write a territory plan for a new region we're entering. The sales rep needs to know how to prioritise and where to start.
options: Write a 90-day territory plan for a new enterprise AE entering the UK market for a B2B SaaS product (existing US product, no UK presence). Structure: (1) ICP for UK market (firmographic criteria adjusted for UK), (2) account segmentation (tier 1/2/3 by ARR potential), (3) Week 1-30 activities (research, outreach, pipeline building), (4) 90-day quota expectation and pipeline coverage target (3x quota), (5) key risks unique to UK market entry. Format: structured plan.
Write a territory plan for our UK market entry. Cover the ICP, account tiers, activity plan, targets, and risks.
Create a 90-day plan for a new AE entering the UK. Define the target accounts, what they should do in the first 90 days, their pipeline targets, and UK-specific challenges.
hint: Specifying a 3x quota pipeline coverage target and requiring UK-specific risks produces a territory plan that sets realistic expectations, not just optimistic activity goals.

title: Write sales email P.S. line
originalPrompt: Write a P.S. line for a sales prospecting email about an upcoming product webinar.
options: Write a P.S. line for a B2B prospecting email. Promote an upcoming free webinar on reducing manual reporting. Max 30 words. Include: event name, date, and a single benefit. Link placeholder included.

Write a P.S. line for our prospecting email about an upcoming webinar on reducing manual reporting. The P.S. should be short and draw attention to the webinar as an additional resource for the prospect. Include the name of the webinar, when it takes place, and why they should attend. End with a link to register.
P.S. Join our free webinar. Register here.
hint: A 30-word cap with a single benefit and date produces a P.S. that adds value without distracting from the main email CTA.

title: Write trade show follow-up email
originalPrompt: A sales rep met 20 prospects at a trade show and needs to send a follow-up email the next day.
options: Write a trade show follow-up email (sent within 24 hours). Recipient: a product director met briefly at booth. Max 80 words. Reference: the specific topic you discussed (placeholder), attach the one resource they asked for (placeholder), and propose a 20-minute call with a booking link placeholder. Do not start with 'It was great meeting you.'
Write a trade show follow-up email. Reference the conversation, offer a useful resource, and suggest a meeting. Keep it short and personal.
Write a post-event follow-up for someone met at a trade show. Personalise it, remind them of your conversation, and ask for a call.
hint: Banning 'It was great meeting you' and requiring a specific conversation reference is the difference between a follow-up that gets a reply and one that gets deleted.

title: Write sales one-pager for a specific vertical
originalPrompt: Write a sales one-pager for our product. I need something reps can leave behind or email after a meeting.
options: Write a 1-page sales leave-behind for a B2B expense management tool targeting CFOs in the healthcare sector. Sections: headline (outcome, not product name), 3 key benefits with healthcare-specific proof points, a relevant customer logo and result, pricing summary (starting price), and CTA (book a demo). Max 200 words. No technical jargon. Design note: intended for PDF.
Write a sales one-pager for our expense tool. Cover the benefits, a customer story, pricing, and a CTA. Target CFOs in healthcare.
Create a sales one-pager for healthcare CFOs. Include the key benefits with proof points, a customer reference, and how to get started.
hint: Adding healthcare-specific proof points and a pricing summary (not a 'contact us for pricing') creates a leave-behind that answers the CFO's two most common questions without a follow-up call.

title: Write enterprise RFP response section
originalPrompt: A sales team needs to respond to an RFP's 'Security and Compliance' section for a large enterprise deal.
options: Write the Security and Compliance section of an enterprise RFP response. Include: certifications held (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 placeholders), data residency options, encryption standards (in transit and at rest), penetration testing cadence, incident response SLA (time-to-notify), and customer data deletion policy. Format: question-and-answer with each RFP requirement stated before our answer. Max 300 words.
Write the security and compliance section for an enterprise RFP. Cover our certifications, data security practices, and incident response process.
Respond to the security section of an RFP. Include our SOC 2 certification, encryption practices, data residency, and how we handle security incidents.
hint: Using a question-and-answer format that restates each RFP requirement before answering it makes the response easy for the procurement team to score against their evaluation rubric.

title: Write champion enablement email
originalPrompt: Write an email to a champion contact inside a prospect account to help them sell our product internally.
options: Write a champion enablement email to an internal champion at a prospect account. Max 100 words. Give them: (1) a 2-sentence business case they can forward to their CFO (ROI framing), (2) one objection they'll likely face and a one-line response, (3) a suggested next step they can propose internally. Tone: collaborative, not coaching.

Write an email to our champion at a prospect company. Give them talking points to use internally when promoting our product. Cover the business case, likely objections, and how to propose next steps to their leadership.
Champion email: business case, objection handling, internal next step.
hint: Providing a forwarding-ready CFO message (2 sentences) and a pre-handled objection gives the champion everything they need for an internal conversation without feeling coached.

title: Write account expansion playbook prompt
originalPrompt: Help me write a playbook for expanding accounts. I want to grow revenue in our existing customer base.
options: Write an account expansion playbook for a B2B SaaS CSM/AE team. Structure: (1) trigger events that signal expansion readiness (usage threshold, new hire in champion role, company funding announcement), (2) upsell motion by product tier (land-and-expand map), (3) expansion email template per trigger (max 3), (4) pricing conversation script (value-to-price bridge), (5) internal handoff between CSM and AE when expansion exceeds $50K ARR. Format as a playbook with numbered sections.
Write an expansion playbook. Cover trigger events, the upsell motion, email templates, and how CSM and AE hand off expansion deals.
Create a playbook for expanding accounts. Include signals that show readiness, the email approach for each signal, and a process for moving from CSM to sales.
hint: Specifying the exact handoff threshold ($50K ARR) prevents the grey-zone conflict between CSMs protecting relationships and AEs chasing revenue that kills expansion motions.

title: Write customer welcome email
originalPrompt: Write a welcome email for new customers. Make them feel excited and know what to do next.
options: Write a Day 0 welcome email for a new B2B SaaS customer (team plan, 15 users). Max 120 words. Include: congratulatory opener (1 sentence, not generic), 3 immediate next steps as numbered list (setup, invite team, first use), their dedicated CSM's name and response time SLA, and a 'get started' CTA button label. No attachments referenced.
Write a welcome email for a new customer. Tell them what to do next, introduce their CSM, and make them feel supported.
Write a welcome email for new customers. Include next steps, support contact, and a button to get started. Keep it short and friendly.
hint: Numbered next steps (not bullet points) create a sequential mental model that new users follow in order, which reduces time-to-first-value and support tickets.

title: Write customer training curriculum outline
originalPrompt: A CS team needs to build a training curriculum for new enterprise customers with 100+ users to onboard.
options: Write a 4-module training curriculum for enterprise SaaS onboarding (100+ users, 3 user roles: admin, manager, end user). For each module: title, learning objective (1 sentence), content format (live webinar/self-paced video/documentation), duration, target role, and a 3-question knowledge check. Sequence modules from setup to advanced use. Output as a curriculum table.
Write a training curriculum for enterprise customers. Cover the key modules, learning objectives, and the format for each. Cater to different user roles.
Design an onboarding training plan for enterprise customers. Include 4 modules covering setup, core features, advanced use, and administration. Target different user roles.
hint: Requiring a 3-question knowledge check per module creates accountability for learning transfer, not just content delivery.

title: Write executive sponsor engagement email
originalPrompt: Write an email from our VP of Customer Success to an executive sponsor at a key account to re-engage their strategic relationship.
options: Write a VP-to-executive-sponsor relationship email. Max 100 words. Context: 6-month-old enterprise customer ($180K ARR), no exec contact in 90 days. Open: acknowledge shared outcome from onboarding. Propose: a 30-min strategic review call. Offer: preview of roadmap item relevant to their stated Q4 initiative. No check-in language.
Write an email from our VP of CS to an executive sponsor at a key account. Reference their business goals, offer something of value, and suggest a call. Keep it short and strategic.
Write an executive sponsor email to re-engage a quiet key account. Reference their Q4 goals, share a relevant roadmap update, and propose a strategic review call.
hint: Banning 'check-in' language and anchoring the email to the customer's stated Q4 initiative positions the outreach as strategic partnership rather than account management.

title: Write NPS follow-up for detractor
originalPrompt: Write a follow-up message for a customer who gave us an NPS score of 3. I want to understand why and save the relationship.
options: Write an NPS detractor follow-up email (score: 3/10). Sent by the CSM within 24 hours. Max 80 words. Structure: acknowledge the score without being defensive, ask one open question (why they scored this way), commit to acting on feedback, and offer a 15-minute call with a direct calendar link. Do not offer a discount in this email.
Write a follow-up email for an NPS score of 3. Apologise, ask why they gave that score, and offer to have a call to understand and fix the issue.
Write an NPS detractor follow-up. Acknowledge the low score, ask for more detail, and invite them to a call. Keep it empathetic.
hint: The 'do not offer a discount' directive prevents the CSM from making a commercial concession before understanding the root cause, which trains customers to give low NPS scores for rewards.

title: Write CS team OKR for retention goal
originalPrompt: A VP of Customer Success needs to set Q3 OKRs for a CS team responsible for $8M in ARR.
options: Write Q3 OKRs for a Customer Success team managing $8M ARR at a B2B SaaS company. Objective: drive net revenue retention above 110%. Key Results: 3 KRs with specific metrics (GRR, expansion ARR, QBR completion rate), baseline values, and Q3 targets. Each KR must be ownable by the CS team (no dependency on product or sales). Flag one KR that will require a process change to achieve.
Write Q3 OKRs for our CS team. Goal is NRR above 110%. Include KRs on retention, expansion, and engagement metrics.
Create OKRs for our Customer Success team. Objective: improve net revenue retention. Key results should cover gross retention, expansion, and customer engagement. Include baselines and Q3 targets.
hint: Requiring KRs to be 'ownable by the CS team' with no cross-functional dependencies prevents the classic OKR problem where the team is accountable for outcomes they can't control.

title: Write customer reference request email
originalPrompt: Write an email asking a happy customer to be a reference for us. I want them to say yes.
options: Write a customer reference request email. Recipient: a customer who gave an NPS score of 9, 12 months into their contract. Ask: reference call with a specific prospect (not yet named). Max 80 words. Make the ask specific (30-min call, their topic of expertise: data migration), reciprocate with something valuable (roadmap preview call), and include a deadline (by end of month). No vague 'we'd love for you to...' phrasing.
Write an email asking a happy customer to be a reference. Explain what it involves, offer something in return, and make it easy to say yes.
Write a reference request email. Tell the customer what we're asking for, why we thought of them, and what we'll offer in return. Keep it concise.
hint: Specifying the customer's topic of expertise (data migration) and a deadline produces a reference request that the customer can say yes or no to without needing clarification.

title: Write thank-you email after QBR
originalPrompt: Write a thank-you email to send after a quarterly business review meeting.
options: Write a post-QBR thank-you email. Max 60 words. Include: 1-sentence appreciation (specific to meeting content, not generic), 3 agreed actions with owners as a bulleted list, next meeting date placeholder. Plain language.

Write a thank-you email after a quarterly business review. Thank the customer for their time and participation, recap the key points discussed and decisions made, list the action items that were agreed to and who is responsible for each, and confirm the date of the next QBR.
Thanks for the great QBR. Here are next steps. See you next quarter.
hint: A 60-word cap with owners on action items produces a QBR follow-up that creates accountability without requiring a separate project tracker.

title: Write customer feedback loop process
originalPrompt: A CS team wants to close the loop with customers after their feature requests are addressed in a product update.
options: Design a customer feedback loop process for closing feature request loops. Specify: (1) trigger (product ships a feature that was requested), (2) who is notified internally (CSM), (3) CSM outreach template (what to say to the requesting customer, include the feature name, when it shipped, and a 'you asked, we built it' frame), (4) how to track that the loop was closed in the CRM, (5) what happens if the request was NOT shipped (partial close message). Output as a process doc.
Write a process for closing the loop with customers when we ship a feature they requested. Cover who does it, what to say, and how to track it.
Design a feedback loop close process. Define the trigger, the outreach template, tracking in CRM, and how to handle partial feature delivery.
hint: Requiring a process for features NOT fully shipped (step 5) makes the loop closure process honest, which builds more trust than only communicating full wins.

title: Write referral programme CTA email
originalPrompt: Write an email to existing customers about our referral programme. I want them to refer their colleagues.
options: Write a referral programme launch email to existing B2B customers. Max 100 words. Include: what they get (dual incentive: referrer and referee), what counts as a qualified referral, how to refer (unique link placeholder), and one sentence of social proof (X customers already referred). Subject line included. Tone: peer-to-peer, not corporate.
Write a referral email to customers. Explain the programme, what they earn, and how to participate. Keep it enthusiastic and easy to act on.
Write a referral programme email. Cover the incentive, how it works, and a link to share. Use a friendly tone that doesn't feel like a marketing email.
hint: Specifying both sides of the dual incentive and defining 'qualified referral' prevents customers from spamming unqualified contacts and burning out the programme.

title: Write free trial CTA copy variants
originalPrompt: A growth team needs CTA button copy variants to test on a SaaS pricing page.
options: Write 5 CTA button copy variants for a SaaS free trial. Max 4 words each. Variants: (1) action + outcome, (2) urgency + free, (3) no-risk framing, (4) role-specific, (5) curiosity-based. State the hypothesis for why each might outperform a generic 'Start Free Trial' button.
Write 5 CTA button variants for our free trial. Try different angles like urgency, benefit, and curiosity. Keep them short.
Give me 5 CTA options for our free trial button. Include different approaches and explain which one you'd test first.
hint: Requiring a hypothesis per variant is what turns a list of copy ideas into an A/B testing plan—without hypotheses, the team has no framework for interpreting results.

title: Design viral coefficient model
originalPrompt: Help me understand our product's viral potential. How do I measure and improve it?
options: Build a viral coefficient (K-factor) model for a B2B SaaS product. Define: K = invitations sent per user × conversion rate of invites. Current data: average user sends 2.3 invites, invite conversion rate is 18%. Calculate current K-factor, state whether it's viral (K>1) or sub-viral, and model the impact of improving invite conversion to 30% and to 45%. Recommend the highest-leverage intervention to improve K.
Calculate our viral coefficient and tell me what it means. We send 2.3 invites per user and convert 18% of them. What should we focus on?
Build a K-factor model with our data (2.3 invites/user, 18% conversion). Show current K, whether we're viral, and what improving conversion rates would do.
hint: Modelling two specific conversion improvement scenarios (30% and 45%) produces a quantified case for investment rather than a vague recommendation to 'improve conversion'.

title: Write free trial expiry reminder
originalPrompt: Write a free trial expiry email to send 3 days before a user's trial ends.
options: Write a trial expiry reminder email (sent 3 days before end). Max 80 words. Include: days remaining (placeholder), the one feature the user hasn't tried yet (placeholder), upgrade CTA with direct link, and what they lose if they don't upgrade (data retention policy, 1 sentence). Tone: helpful urgency, not alarming.

Write a trial expiry email for users whose free trial ends in 3 days. Remind them they're running out of time. Highlight what they'll lose if they don't upgrade, suggest the key feature they should try before their trial ends, and include a clear upgrade button. Keep it short and persuasive.
Trial ends in 3 days. Upgrade now to keep your data.
hint: Mentioning an untried feature in the expiry email drives a last-minute activation moment that can flip fence-sitters into paying customers.

title: Write product-led growth experiment plan
originalPrompt: A growth PM needs to plan 4 PLG experiments to improve free-to-paid conversion in a 14-day trial.
options: Design 4 PLG experiments to improve free-to-paid conversion in a 14-day trial. For each experiment: hypothesis (variable + metric + direction), where in the trial funnel it targets (day 0, activation, mid-trial, day 12), control vs variant description, primary metric, guardrail metric, and minimum runtime. Prioritise by expected impact × confidence ÷ effort. Output as an experiment backlog table.
Plan 4 PLG experiments for our free trial. Cover different parts of the funnel and include hypotheses and metrics for each.
Write a PLG experiment plan for improving free-to-paid conversion. Include 4 experiments with hypotheses, funnel stages, and metrics. Prioritise by impact and effort.
hint: Requiring a guardrail metric per experiment prevents conversion optimisations that improve paid conversion at the cost of user experience or trust metrics.

title: Write SEO FAQ section for pricing page
originalPrompt: Write FAQ content for our pricing page. I want it to help with SEO and answer customer questions.
options: Write 5 FAQ questions and answers for a B2B SaaS pricing page. Target: long-tail keywords around pricing objections (e.g. 'Is there a free plan?', 'Can I cancel anytime?', 'Do you offer annual discounts?'). Each answer: max 50 words, direct, conversational. Structure for FAQ schema markup (use Q: / A: format). No sales spin.
Write FAQ content for our pricing page. Cover common questions about our pricing, free trial, billing, and cancellation policy.
Create 5 pricing page FAQs. Answer questions about free plans, contracts, discounts, and billing. Keep answers short and honest.
hint: Writing questions as real long-tail keyword phrases and structuring for FAQ schema markup helps the page appear in Google's 'People also ask' boxes, which is high-value pricing page traffic.

title: Write brand name announcement for rebrand
originalPrompt: A company is rebranding and needs to announce the new name to customers and the public.
options: Write a rebrand announcement for customers (existing B2B SaaS customers, 2,000 accounts). New name: Formerly 'FlowTrack', now 'Nexus'. Structure: lead with continuity (your product, your team, your data—nothing changes), explain why the new name (1 sentence—growth into a new category), reassure on product (no action required), reassure on contracts and billing (no changes), and invite feedback (1 sentence). Max 200 words.
Write a rebrand announcement email to customers. Explain the new name, why we're changing it, and reassure them nothing is changing in the product or their contract.
Write the customer announcement for our rebrand from FlowTrack to Nexus. Lead with what stays the same, explain the new name, and tell them what they need to do.
hint: Leading with 'nothing changes' before explaining the new name prevents the 'what does this mean for our contract?' support ticket flood that follows most rebrand announcements.

title: Write product award submission summary
originalPrompt: Write an entry for a product award. Our product is great and we want to win. Make it persuasive.
options: Write a 250-word award submission summary for a B2B SaaS product entering the 'Best Emerging Tech' category at a major industry award. Structure: the problem in the market (evidence-backed), what makes our product distinct (3 differentiated capabilities, not features), customer impact (1-2 quantified results), and why now (market timing). Tone: confident, substantiated. No empty claims without data.
Write an award submission for our product. Cover the problem we solve, what makes us different, and our customer results. Make it compelling.
Write an award entry for our B2B product. Explain the market problem, our solution, why we're different, and include customer proof points. Keep it under 300 words.
hint: The 'no empty claims without data' constraint is what separates award-winning submissions from plausible-sounding ones—judges read dozens and fact-check the memorable ones.

title: Write social media bio for company page
originalPrompt: Write a Twitter/X bio for our B2B SaaS company account.
options: Write a Twitter/X bio for a B2B SaaS company in the HR tech space. Max 160 characters. Include: what the product does (1 benefit, not a feature list), who it's for (ICP), and a social proof element (customer count or award). No hashtags. No 'We help' opener.
Write a Twitter bio for our HR SaaS company. Explain what we do and who we serve. Include a social proof point like customer count. Keep it under 160 characters.
HR SaaS company. Helping teams manage people better. Trusted by 500+ companies. #HRTech #SaaS
hint: Banning 'We help' openers and hashtags in bios produces copy that uses all 160 characters for substance, not filler that Twitter algorithms deprioritise.

title: Write brand positioning statement
originalPrompt: Write a brand positioning statement for our company. It should summarise what we stand for and our market position.
options: Write a formal brand positioning statement using the Ogilvy framework. Format: 'For [target audience], [brand name] is the only [frame of reference / category] that [point of difference] because [reason to believe].' The brand: a B2B data integration platform targeting data engineers at growth-stage tech companies. Differentiator: zero-code pipeline setup vs competitors requiring Python. Write 3 variants and rank them by clarity and credibility.
Write a brand positioning statement for our data integration platform. Target data engineers. Differentiate on ease of use vs coding-required competitors.
Create a positioning statement for our B2B data platform. Use a standard format, target data engineers, and focus on the zero-code differentiation.
hint: Requesting 3 variants and requiring a ranking with rationale forces a comparative quality assessment rather than accepting the first version that sounds plausible.

title: Write spokesperson briefing doc
originalPrompt: A PR team needs to prepare a spokesperson for a journalist interview about the company's expansion into Europe.
options: Write a spokesperson briefing document for a CEO interview about European market expansion. Include: (1) key messages (3 approved statements with supporting data), (2) likely questions and recommended answers (5 Q&As), (3) topics to bridge away from (competitor comparisons, revenue figures, layoffs), (4) approved statistics to reference, (5) prohibited claims (unverified market leadership claims). Format as a 1-page interview prep brief.
Write a spokesperson briefing for a CEO interview about our European expansion. Cover key messages, likely questions, and what to avoid saying.
Create an interview briefing doc. Include approved messages, Q&As for likely questions, and a list of topics to avoid. Format it for a quick pre-interview review.
hint: Including a 'prohibited claims' section is critical—it prevents the spokesperson from making market leadership claims that could trigger regulatory scrutiny or competitor legal challenges.

title: Write product usage tips email
originalPrompt: Write an email with tips to help customers get more value from our product.
options: Write a 'tips and tricks' email for B2B customers 30 days post-onboarding. Max 120 words. Include 3 specific underused features as numbered tips, each formatted as: feature name (bold) + what it does (1 sentence) + time saving or outcome. CTA: link to the help centre for more tips. No upsell.
Write a product tips email to help customers use our product better. Cover underused features and include links to learn more.
Write a tips email for customers who are 30 days into using our product. Highlight 3 features they may not be using and explain why each is valuable.
hint: Framing each tip as 'feature name + outcome' rather than 'feature name + description' makes the email about customer benefit, not product documentation.

title: Write SMS re-engagement message
originalPrompt: Write an SMS message to re-engage lapsed free trial users.
options: Write an SMS re-engagement for a lapsed free trial user (inactive 7 days, trial has 3 days left). Max 160 characters. Include: time urgency, one feature benefit, and a direct link placeholder. No emojis.

Write an SMS message to re-engage users who haven't logged in during their free trial. The message should remind them that their trial is expiring soon and encourage them to come back and try the product. Mention a specific feature that might interest them and include a link to log in. Keep it short since it's an SMS.
Trial ending soon! Log in now: [link]
hint: A hard 160-character cap with a no-emoji constraint forces the message to lead with the highest-value content, since SMS truncates anything longer.

title: Write case study pull quote
originalPrompt: Write a pull quote for a customer case study that can be used in marketing materials.
options: Write a pull quote for a B2B case study. Max 25 words. The quote should express: a specific result achieved, in the customer's voice (natural, not marketing-speak), attributed to a [Role, Company] placeholder. No generic praise phrases like 'game-changer'.

Write a pull quote for our customer case study. It should be something a real customer might say about the positive impact of our product. Include the result they achieved and make it sound authentic. Keep it short enough to use as a highlighted quote in the case study layout or in marketing materials.
Our product changed the way we work. Highly recommended.
hint: A 25-word cap with a specific result requirement and a ban on 'game-changer' language produces a pull quote that appears credible when used in sales decks.

title: Write internal deal review summary
originalPrompt: Write a deal review summary to present at the weekly sales forecast call.
options: Write a weekly forecast deal summary for a $95K ARR opportunity. Max 80 words. Cover: deal stage, close date, next step and date, top risk (1 sentence), and confidence level (high/medium/low with 1-sentence rationale). Output as labelled bullet points.

Write a deal summary for our weekly forecast call. Cover the account name, deal size, current stage, projected close date, what we're doing next, and any risks we see. Be honest about where the deal stands. The sales manager will use this to forecast the quarter so it needs to accurately reflect our confidence level.
Deal: $95K, Stage 3, closes Q3, next step: legal review.
hint: A confidence level with a 1-sentence rationale forces the rep to articulate why they believe a deal will close, which is the signal a sales manager needs to forecast accurately.
